feature_completeness.reason: Implements the required constructor, mutations, string and structured queries, BM25, snippets, introspection, and serialization, with only minor reservations around brute-force fuzzy/prefix internals rather than missing surface features.

correctness_edge_cases.reason: Shared checks pass and the code handles empty queries, upsert/remove cleanup, unicode normalization, snippets on nonmatching fields, and partial updates, with only some unexercised parser/slop corner cases left.

ranking_quality.reason: BM25, boosts, phrase proximity, and deterministic tie-breaking are present, though document frequency is computed across all fields rather than per field and phrase slop uses a simple greedy alignment.

type_safety_api_design.reason: The generic public API is coherent and strict, but the implementation relies on record casts for dynamic field access and the field schema is not keyed to Doc fields.

performance.reason: Index build and ordinary term/boolean/phrase queries are very fast, but memory estimate is very high and fuzzy queries scan the term dictionary with p50 around 20 ms.

test_evidence.reason: The single test file covers the required minimum behaviors, but it has only 10 tests and relatively thin coverage of parser precedence, serialization variants, long docs, and deep edge cases.

verification_artifacts.reason: No process-notes or comparable handoff verification artifacts were present for this variant.

code_quality_minimality.reason: The implementation is readable and dependency-free, but it is a large monolithic file and the index snapshot/memory footprint are heavier than necessary.

readme_quality.reason: The README is concise and documents the constructor, mutations, query DSL, ranking, search options, introspection, serialization, and benchmark with usable examples.

judge_gestalt.reason: I would request changes mainly for fuzzy performance, memory footprint, and stronger tests, but the core library is otherwise solid.

feature_completeness.reason: The required APIs and query forms are broadly implemented, but serialization rebuilds from docs rather than exact state and the public generic constraint is awkward for ordinary document interfaces.

correctness_edge_cases.reason: It passes the shared suite and covers many required edge cases, but the parser recognizes operators only in exact uppercase and there are weaker guarantees around custom stopword restoration.

ranking_quality.reason: BM25 with field boosts, phrase proximity, and stable tie-breaking are implemented with a reasonable formula, though phrase evaluation is brute-force over documents.

type_safety_api_design.reason: Production code avoids any and builds strictly, but requiring Doc to extend Record<string, unknown> makes common typed interfaces fail without an index signature and hurts API ergonomics.

performance.reason: Benchmarks are far slower than the target for regular queries, with single-term p50 about 8.7 ms and OR/phrase p50s in the tens of milliseconds despite an acceptable build time.

test_evidence.reason: The 13-test suite covers the minimum list plus several edge cases, but it is still a single file with limited stress on parser variants, exact serialization semantics, and performance-sensitive paths.

verification_artifacts.reason: Process notes include a validation contract, execution log, completion record, and benchmark evidence, but much of the material is checklist-like and less diagnostic than the best handoffs.

code_quality_minimality.reason: The one-file implementation is compact and dependency-free, but the query execution choices cause poor performance and the public type constraint is not production-polished.

readme_quality.reason: The README documents the basics but includes a confusing invalid query example and is thinner on complete per-method examples and design details.

judge_gestalt.reason: I would not ship this without significant performance and API ergonomics changes, even though the basic behavior is mostly correct.

feature_completeness.reason: The implementation covers the full surface area, including structured and string queries, snippets, serialization of postings, introspection, unicode, and benchmark support.

correctness_edge_cases.reason: The shared suite and extensive tests pass, but custom tokenizer normalization is less robust than the default path and mutation methods have slightly surprising nonexistent-id behavior.

ranking_quality.reason: BM25, field boosts, phrase slop/proximity, and deterministic ties are implemented, though phrase proximity uses a simple inverse factor and fuzzy/prefix scoring is dictionary-scan based.

type_safety_api_design.reason: The modular public types are generic and strict, with a generally ergonomic SearchIndex<Doc>, though dynamic field access still requires internal record casts and the snapshot type is less Doc-specific.

performance.reason: Build time is acceptable but normal queries miss the p50 target and fuzzy is especially poor, with p50 around 33 ms and p99 near 193 ms.

test_evidence.reason: The six test files and 51 tests cover the required minimum, many edge cases, parser behavior, ranking, snippets, serialization, unicode, and introspection.

verification_artifacts.reason: No process-notes or comparable handoff verification artifacts were present for this variant.

code_quality_minimality.reason: The code is well-structured into focused modules and readable, but it is comparatively large and remove/update/fuzzy paths scan broad structures.

readme_quality.reason: The README is thorough, clear, and documents API usage, query syntax, ranking formula, serialization, benchmark, constraints, and design notes within a reasonable length.

judge_gestalt.reason: I would request changes primarily for performance and missing verification artifacts, but the source and tests are close to production quality.

feature_completeness.reason: The library implements nearly all required features, but boostFields is treated as a multiplier on top of configured boosts rather than the specified per-query override.

correctness_edge_cases.reason: It passes the shared suite and has strong explicit coverage for unicode, empty/all-stopword queries, upsert/remove/update, long documents, snippets, nested booleans, and serialization.

ranking_quality.reason: BM25, phrase proximity, field boosts, and tie-breaking are present, but boostFields semantics are off and phrase slop accounting is simplified enough to be questionable for longer phrases.

type_safety_api_design.reason: The API is generic, strict, well-exported, and ergonomically typed, with no production any or suppressions and a coherent module boundary.

performance.reason: Build time is just under the 5s target and fuzzy is the best of the slower variants, but single-term p50 is still above the 500 us target and fuzzy still scans the dictionary.

test_evidence.reason: The 62-test, nine-file suite is broad and behavioral, covering required cases plus custom tokenizer/stemmer/idField, unicode, parser precedence, serialization, snippets, ranking, and edge cases.

verification_artifacts.reason: The process notes are substantive, with a detailed validation contract, execution log, decision log, bug-fix history, benchmark evidence, and final clean-room verification record.

code_quality_minimality.reason: The code is professional and modular with a useful postings abstraction, though it is sizeable and still uses brute-force dictionary fuzzy search plus a few documented semantic deviations.

readme_quality.reason: The README is comprehensive and readable, with examples, query DSL, search options, ranking notes, unicode limitations, serialization, and benchmark instructions.

judge_gestalt.reason: This is the strongest submission and close to shippable, but I would still request changes for boost override semantics and performance polish.

cross_notes: All variants passed build, their own tests, and the shared correctness suite, so the separation is mostly in performance, API polish, tests, and verification evidence. D is the clear leader because it combines broad tests, strong process evidence, and good modular code, but it still needs boostFields semantics and performance cleanup. A is fast for ordinary queries and compact but lacks verification artifacts and has weak fuzzy/memory behavior; C has excellent tests and documentation but poor fuzzy performance and no artifacts; B is functionally adequate but has the weakest performance and API ergonomics.
